one_line_takeaway: VIGIL reframes agent defense against tool-stream prompt injection as speculative, sandboxed hypothesis generation followed by two-stage LLM verification (constraint compliance + intent entailment) before any action commits, cutting tool-stream ASR to roughly 8-12% and more than doubling utility-under-attack versus static plan-then-execute and prior dynamic baselines on the paper's own SIREN benchmark.
key_finding: On Qwen3-max, VIGIL reduced average tool-stream ASR from CaMeL's roughly 25% down to about 8% (and to about 12% on Gemini-2.5-pro), and the paper states this surpasses the best dynamic baseline (DRIFT) by a margin of 22-24 percentage points across both backbones. From Table 2 (Qwen3-max, Tool Stream Overall column): VIGIL achieves UA=27.53%/ASR=8.13%, versus CaMeL UA=11.68%/ASR=25.34%, MELON UA=12.00%/ASR=36.70%, DRIFT UA=13.33%/ASR=32.64%, and undefended Vanilla ReAct UA=15.95%/ASR=73.83%. VIGIL's Benign Utility on Qwen3-max was 74.49%, close to Vanilla ReAct's 79.59%, while CaMeL's BU fell to 46.79% and DeBERTa's to 43.88%. On the data-stream baseline (Qwen3-max), VIGIL's ASR was 0.32%, comparable to Tool-Filter's 0.11% and better than CaMeL's 0.00%-tied but far lower utility case, and better than DRIFT's 59.75% and MELON's 35.63%. Per-vector Explicit Directive results show CaMeL's ASR near 44.83% (paper text rounds this to 'nearly 45%') due to 'context contamination' from static planning, versus VIGIL's 16.09%. Ablation (Table 3, tool stream): removing the Grounding Verifier (Unverified) caused ASR to spike to 45.05% (from the full system's 8.13%); removing the Speculative Reasoner (Linear) caused UA to collapse from 27.53% to 9.07%, while its ASR stayed comparatively low (8.45%) -- illustrating the two components independently guard security and utility respectively. Scalability analysis (Figure 4a/b) found verification rounds/time converge to a roughly constant level even when the toolset grew from 496 to 3,074 tools, attributed to trajectory-memory caching. Attack-density analysis (Figure 4c) found ASR stayed low from 1:1 to 1:8 malicious:benign ratios while UA declined gradually rather than collapsing.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold: (1) SIREN, a new benchmark of 959 tool-stream indirect prompt injection cases across five attack vectors (Explicit Directive, Dependency Trap, Feature Inducement, Runtime Hijacking, Error Hijacking), built on top of AgentDojo's environment with an expanded 496-tool library and stochastic runtime failures, plus 949 data-stream cases reused from AgentDojo as a baseline; and (2) VIGIL (Verifiable Intent-Grounded Interaction Loop), a defense framework that replaces the rigid 'plan-then-execute' isolation paradigm with a 'verify-before-commit' loop. VIGIL has five components: an Intent Anchor that derives immutable logical invariants (constraints C) from the user query via a role-specialized LLM; a Perception Sanitizer that rewrites tool docstrings/feedback into objective factual statements, stripping imperative/coercive language before it reaches the reasoning model; a Speculative Reasoner that explores multiple candidate action trajectories inside a 'hypothetical sandbox' without committing any action; a Grounding Verifier that checks each candidate trajectory in two sequential stages -- invariant compliance (matching action metadata against the hard constraints C) followed by semantic entailment (is the action logically necessary to fulfill user intent q); and a Validated Trajectory Memory that caches approved trajectories and triggers reflective backtracking on verification failure.
threat_model: Standard black-box threat model (per the paper, following Zhang et al. 2024a and Zhu et al. 2025): the agent's system instructions and private memory sit inside a trust boundary; the external environment (data retrieved and tools invoked) is untrusted. The adversary has no access to model weights/internal state and can only influence the agent by manipulating retrieved information. The paper's specific extension is widening the attack surface from the passive data stream (e.g., malicious content in emails/webpages) to the active tool stream, treating the adversary as a compromised third-party tool provider who can inject malicious constraints into tool definitions/docstrings during the planning phase and fabricate deceptive error messages/status feedback during the execution phase, mimicking authoritative system commands to override user intent.
limitations: Limitations stated explicitly by the authors: (1) VIGIL's security depends on a speculative reasoning-verification loop, so exploring a large hypothesis space for complex tasks can introduce significant computational overhead (acknowledged as needing future work on lightweight verifiers/pruning); (2) the framework's security is grounded in immutable constraints derived from the initial user query, which may limit adaptability to open-ended tasks where sub-goals emerge dynamically from retrieved data -- i.e., the intent-anchoring approach is static once query-derived constraints are set, even though the rest of the pipeline is designed to be adaptive. Additional grounding-derived observations not phrased as limitations by the authors but visible in the results: on Gemini-2.5-pro, VIGIL's BU (40.82%) is noticeably lower than Vanilla ReAct's (65.31%), described by the authors as 'a moderate performance trade-off ... due to the conservative nature of the verifier' -- i.e., real utility cost on at least one backbone. VIGIL's absolute tool-stream UA remains low in absolute terms even where it leads (27.53% best case on Qwen3-max, 18.46% on Gemini-2.5-pro under Tool Stream Overall), meaning even the best defense still fails to complete/neutralize the majority of adversarial tasks. The Grounding Verifier and Intent Anchor both rely on LLM-based judgment (role-specialized LLM calls) for compliance/entailment checks, which the paper does not stress-test against adversarial inputs specifically targeting the verifier or anchor themselves (i.e., no adaptive/white-box attack against VIGIL's own components is evaluated).

datasets_used: SIREN (Systemic Injection & Reasoning Evaluation beNchmark) -- the paper's own new benchmark, 959 tool-stream injection cases across 5 vectors, built on a reconstructed AgentDojo environment expanded to 496 tools
AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al., 2024) -- reused as the environment base and as the source of 949 data-stream content-injection baseline cases
quantitative_results: Tool-stream ASR (Qwen3-max, Tool Stream Overall): VIGIL 8.13% vs Vanilla ReAct 73.83%, CaMeL 25.34%, MELON 36.70%, DRIFT 32.64%, Tool-Filter 20.13%, Spotlighting 63.61%, DeBERTa 47.24%
Tool-stream UA (Qwen3-max, Tool Stream Overall): VIGIL 27.53% vs CaMeL 11.68%, MELON 12.00%, DRIFT 13.33%, Tool-Filter 5.11%, Vanilla ReAct 15.95%
Benign Utility (Qwen3-max): VIGIL 74.49% vs Vanilla ReAct 79.59%, CaMeL 46.79%, DeBERTa 43.88%
Explicit Directive vector ASR (Qwen3-max): CaMeL ~44.83% vs VIGIL 16.09%

Ablation (tool stream): full system ASR 8.13%/UA 27.53%; Unverified variant (no Grounding Verifier) ASR 45.05%/UA 13.76%; Linear variant (no Speculative Reasoner) ASR 8.45%/UA 9.07%; Unanchored ASR 15.33%/UA 21.58%; Unfiltered ASR 24.19%/UA 18.56%
Gemini-2.5-pro Tool Stream Overall: VIGIL UA 18.46%/ASR 11.99%/BU 40.82%, vs Vanilla ReAct UA 12.93%/ASR 58.92%/BU 65.31%
SIREN attack vector case counts: Explicit Directive 174, Dependency Trap 184, Feature Inducement 179, Runtime Hijacking 272, Error Hijacking 150 (total 959); data-stream baseline 949 cases
baselines_compared: Spotlighting (Hines et al. 2024, delimiter-based prompt augmentation)
Tool-Filter (Debenedetti et al. 2024, query-based tool whitelisting)
CaMeL (Debenedetti et al. 2025, strict plan-then-execute isolation)
MELON (Zhu et al. 2025, masked re-execution anomaly detection)
DRIFT (Li et al. 2025a, dynamic rule-based policy updates)
